question_data.question: Article 9 of the Constitution of Pakistan 1973 deals with:
question_data.explanation: Article 9 states: No person shall be deprived of life or liberty save in accordance with law.

question_data.question: Under the Constitution of Pakistan, the power to amend the Constitution lies with:
question_data.explanation: Article 239 of the Constitution grants the Parliament the power to amend the Constitution by a two-thirds majority.

question_data.question: Which article provides for writ jurisdiction of the High Court?
question_data.explanation: Article 199 empowers each High Court to issue writs including habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto, and certiorari.

question_data.question: The President of Pakistan can dissolve the National Assembly under:
question_data.explanation: Article 58 deals with the dissolution of the National Assembly on the advice of the Prime Minister.

question_data.question: Fundamental Rights in the Constitution of Pakistan 1973 are contained in:
question_data.explanation: Fundamental Rights are enshrined in Part II, Chapter 1 (Articles 8-28) of the Constitution.

question_data.question: The State religion of Pakistan under Article 2 is:
question_data.explanation: Article 2 declares Islam as the State religion of Pakistan.

question_data.question: Article 10-A (Right to Fair Trial) was inserted by which Constitutional Amendment?
question_data.explanation: The 18th Amendment (2010) inserted Article 10-A guaranteeing the right to fair trial and due process.

question_data.question: The Objectives Resolution was made a substantive part of the Constitution through:
question_data.explanation: Article 2-A states the Objectives Resolution is a substantive part of the Constitution.

question_data.question: Under Article 184(3), the Supreme Court can take suo motu notice of matters involving:
question_data.explanation: Article 184(3) allows the SC to take up matters of public importance involving enforcement of fundamental rights.

question_data.question: The total number of members in the Senate of Pakistan is:
question_data.explanation: The Senate consists of 96 members with equal representation from each province.

question_data.question: The Federal Shariat Court is established under which Article?
question_data.explanation: Article 203-C establishes the Federal Shariat Court to examine whether laws are repugnant to Islam.

question_data.question: The Concurrent Legislative List was abolished by which Amendment?
question_data.explanation: The 18th Amendment (2010) abolished the Concurrent Legislative List, devolving subjects to provinces.

question_data.question: Proclamation of Emergency under the Constitution can be made by:
question_data.explanation: Under Article 232, the President may proclaim a state of emergency when the security of Pakistan is threatened.

question_data.question: Under Article 63, a person is disqualified from Parliament if:
options: He has been convicted of moral turpitude
question_data.explanation: Article 63 lists grounds including conviction involving moral turpitude, insolvency, and dual nationality.

question_data.question: The 18th Amendment transferred which subjects from Federal to Provincial list?
question_data.explanation: The 18th Amendment transferred 47 subjects including education, health, environment to provinces.

question_data.question: Article 25 of the Constitution guarantees:
question_data.explanation: Article 25 states all citizens are equal before law and entitled to equal protection of law.

question_data.question: Article 25-A guarantees free education for children aged:
question_data.explanation: Article 25-A provides for free and compulsory education to all children aged 5-16 years.

question_data.question: Article 19-A provides citizens the right to:
question_data.explanation: Article 19-A guarantees every citizen the right to have access to information in matters of public importance.

question_data.question: The total number of general seats in the National Assembly is:
question_data.explanation: The National Assembly has 342 total seats including 272 general seats and reserved seats for women and minorities.

question_data.question: The minimum age to become Prime Minister of Pakistan is:
question_data.explanation: Under Article 91, a member of the National Assembly can become PM, and the minimum age for NA membership is 25.

question_data.question: Section 302 of the PPC deals with:
question_data.explanation: Section 302 PPC prescribes punishment for Qatl-i-amd (intentional murder).

question_data.question: Under PPC, the right of private defence extends to causing death in cases under:
question_data.explanation: Section 100 lists situations where private defence extends to causing death.

question_data.question: Under Section 497 CrPC, bail in non-bailable offences requires:
question_data.explanation: Section 497 allows bail when there are reasonable grounds for believing the accused is not guilty.

question_data.question: An FIR is registered under which section of CrPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 154 CrPC provides for information in cognizable cases to be recorded as FIR.

question_data.question: Section 144 CrPC empowers which authority to impose restrictions?
question_data.explanation: Section 144 empowers the District Magistrate to issue orders in urgent cases of nuisance or danger.

question_data.question: Dishonour of cheque is punishable under which section of PPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 489-F PPC punishes dishonest issuance of a cheque that is dishonoured with up to 3 years imprisonment.

question_data.question: Section 420 PPC deals with:
options: Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property
question_data.explanation: Section 420 punishes cheating and thereby dishonestly inducing delivery of any property.

question_data.question: Section 34 PPC deals with:
question_data.explanation: Section 34 provides that when a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of common intention, each is liable.

question_data.question: Criminal conspiracy is defined under which section of PPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 120-B defines and punishes criminal conspiracy.

question_data.question: How many persons are required to constitute dacoity?
question_data.explanation: Under Section 395, when five or more persons conjointly commit robbery, it is dacoity.

question_data.question: Diyat (blood money) is defined under which section of PPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 308 defines diyat and provides that its value shall not be less than 30,630 grams of silver.

question_data.question: Pre-arrest/anticipatory bail is provided under which section of CrPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 498 CrPC provides for pre-arrest bail when a person apprehends arrest in a non-bailable offence.

question_data.question: Police report/challan is submitted under which section of CrPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 173 CrPC requires the police officer to forward a report (challan) to the Magistrate upon completion of investigation.

question_data.question: Recording of confession before a Magistrate is governed by:
question_data.explanation: Section 164 CrPC provides for recording of confessions and statements before a Magistrate.

question_data.question: Habeas corpus can be issued by the High Court under which section of CrPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 491 CrPC empowers the High Court to issue habeas corpus for persons illegally detained.

question_data.question: Inherent powers of the High Court are preserved under:
question_data.explanation: Section 561-A preserves the inherent powers of the High Court to make orders necessary for justice or prevent abuse of process.

question_data.question: Defiling the Holy Quran is punishable under which section of PPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 295-B PPC provides for punishment of willful defiling of the Holy Quran.

question_data.question: Minimum punishment for rape under Section 376 PPC is:
question_data.explanation: Section 376 provides imprisonment not less than 10 years but may extend to 25 years or death.

question_data.question: Compounding of offences is dealt with under which section of CrPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 345 CrPC lists offences that may be compounded by specified persons.

question_data.question: Cyber terrorism under PECA 2016 is punishable with imprisonment up to:
question_data.explanation: Section 10 of PECA provides imprisonment up to 14 years or fine up to 50 million rupees for cyber terrorism.

question_data.question: The principle of Res Judicata is contained in which section of CPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 11 CPC embodies the principle of Res Judicata preventing re-litigation of decided issues.

question_data.question: The limitation period for a suit for recovery of money is:
question_data.explanation: Under Article 46 of the Limitation Act, the period is 3 years from when the amount becomes due.

question_data.question: Inherent powers of the court are saved under which section of CPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 151 CPC saves the inherent power of the Court to make orders necessary for the ends of justice.

question_data.question: A second appeal lies to the High Court under which section of CPC?
question_data.explanation: Section 100 CPC provides for second appeal to the High Court on substantial questions of law.

question_data.question: Revisional jurisdiction of the High Court is under which section?
question_data.explanation: Section 115 CPC provides revisional jurisdiction to the High Court.

question_data.question: Section 10 CPC deals with:
question_data.explanation: Section 10 stays proceedings when the same matter is directly in issue in a previously instituted suit.

question_data.question: Every suit is instituted by presentation of a:
question_data.explanation: Section 26 CPC provides that every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a plaint.

question_data.question: Under CPC, after hearing a case the court pronounces:
question_data.explanation: Section 33 CPC states the court shall pronounce judgment and a decree shall follow.

question_data.question: Under Article 117 of QSO, the burden of proof lies on:
question_data.explanation: Article 117 states the burden lies on the person who would fail if no evidence were given on either side.

question_data.question: A dying declaration is admissible under which Article of QSO?
question_data.explanation: Article 37 QSO deals with dying declarations about the cause of death.

question_data.question: The competence and number of witnesses is governed by which Article of QSO?
question_data.explanation: Article 17 determines competence of witnesses according to Islamic injunctions.

question_data.question: Under QSO, all facts except contents of documents may be proved by:
question_data.explanation: Article 59 states all facts except contents of documents may be proved by oral evidence.

question_data.question: Cross-examination of a witness is conducted by:
options: The party who called the witness
question_data.explanation: Article 133 defines cross-examination as examination of a witness by the adverse party.

question_data.question: The principle of estoppel is contained in which Article of QSO?
question_data.explanation: Article 153 QSO embodies the doctrine of estoppel.

question_data.question: A question suggesting the desired answer is called a:
question_data.explanation: Article 140 defines a leading question as one that suggests the answer expected.

question_data.question: Admissions are defined under which Article of QSO?
question_data.explanation: Article 27 defines an admission as a statement suggesting inference as to a fact in issue.

question_data.question: Under QSO, the court shall presume genuineness of:
question_data.explanation: Article 76 QSO creates a presumption of genuineness for certified copies of court records.

question_data.question: Under Section 10 of the Contract Act, which is NOT essential for a valid contract?
question_data.explanation: A written document is not always required. Section 10 requires free consent, competent parties, lawful consideration, and lawful object.

question_data.question: An agreement in restraint of trade under Section 27 is:
question_data.explanation: Section 27 declares every agreement in restraint of trade void, except for sale of goodwill.

question_data.question: Under the Contract Act, a person competent to contract must be:
question_data.explanation: Section 11 states every person of the age of majority and of sound mind is competent to contract.

question_data.question: Coercion under Section 15 of the Contract Act includes:
options: Threatening an act forbidden by PPC
question_data.explanation: Section 15 defines coercion as committing or threatening to commit any act forbidden by the PPC.

question_data.question: Fraud is defined under which section of the Contract Act?
question_data.explanation: Section 17 defines fraud as acts committed with intent to deceive another party.

question_data.question: An agreement to do an impossible act is:
question_data.explanation: Section 56 states an agreement to do an act impossible in itself is void.

question_data.question: Compensation for breach of contract is governed by Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 73 provides that the party suffering breach is entitled to compensation for loss or damage.

question_data.question: A contract of guarantee involves how many parties?
question_data.explanation: Section 126 defines guarantee as a contract involving three parties: surety, principal debtor, and creditor.

question_data.question: Bailment is defined under which section of the Contract Act?
question_data.explanation: Section 148 defines bailment as delivery of goods by one person to another for some purpose.

question_data.question: An agent is defined under Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 182 defines an agent as a person employed to do any act for another.

question_data.question: Khula is a form of dissolution of marriage at the instance of:
question_data.explanation: Khula is the wife's right to seek dissolution by returning the dower to the husband.

question_data.question: Under Section 7 of MFLO, talaq notice must be given to:
options: The Chairman and a copy to the wife
question_data.explanation: Section 7 requires notice to the Chairman of Union Council and a copy to the wife. Talaq is effective after 90 days.

question_data.question: Under MFLO, a man requires permission for second marriage from:
question_data.explanation: Section 6 of MFLO requires previous permission of the Arbitration Council for a second marriage.

question_data.question: Under the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939, a wife can seek dissolution on:
options: Specified grounds including cruelty, desertion, and non-maintenance
question_data.explanation: Section 2 of DMMA lists specific grounds including whereabouts unknown, failure to maintain, imprisonment, impotency, insanity, cruelty.

question_data.question: Under the Child Marriage Restraint Act, the minimum marriage age for boys is:
question_data.explanation: The minimum age is 18 for boys (16 for girls in most provinces).

question_data.question: Under Section 10 of MFLO, when no mode of payment of dower is specified, the entire amount is presumed:
question_data.explanation: Section 10 states the entire dower amount is presumed payable on demand if no payment mode is specified.

question_data.question: Under the Guardians and Wards Act, the paramount consideration for appointing a guardian is:
question_data.explanation: Section 17 GWA states the court shall consider the welfare of the minor as the paramount consideration.

question_data.question: Succession for non-Muslims in Pakistan is governed by:
question_data.explanation: The Succession Act 1925 governs testamentary and intestate succession for non-Muslims.

question_data.question: Under TPA, sale is defined as transfer of ownership for:
question_data.explanation: Section 54 TPA defines sale as transfer of ownership in exchange for a price.

question_data.question: A mortgage under TPA is defined in Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 58 defines mortgage as transfer of interest in immovable property to secure payment of money.

question_data.question: Under Section 123 TPA, a gift of immovable property requires:
question_data.explanation: Section 123 states gift of immovable property must be registered.

question_data.question: The doctrine of Lis Pendens is contained in which section of TPA?
question_data.explanation: Section 52 TPA embodies lis pendens - property subject of a suit cannot be transferred to defeat rights of parties.

question_data.question: A lease under TPA is defined in Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 105 defines lease as transfer of right to enjoy property for a certain time for rent.

question_data.question: The right of pre-emption (shuf'a) in Punjab is governed by:
question_data.explanation: The Punjab Pre-emption Act 1991 grants pre-emption rights to co-sharers and neighbours.

question_data.question: Under Section 17 of the Registration Act, registration is compulsory for:
options: Instruments of gift and transfer of immovable property over Rs. 100
question_data.explanation: Section 17 requires registration of gifts of immovable property and transfers exceeding Rs. 100.

question_data.question: Compensation for land acquisition is determined under Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 23 of the Land Acquisition Act lists matters to be considered in determining compensation.

question_data.question: The primary sources of Islamic law are:
question_data.explanation: The four primary sources are: Quran, Sunnah (Hadith), Ijma (consensus), and Qiyas (analogical reasoning).

question_data.question: The predominant school of Islamic jurisprudence in Pakistan is:
question_data.explanation: The Hanafi school is the predominant school of jurisprudence followed in Pakistan.

question_data.question: Hudood refers to:
question_data.explanation: Hudood are fixed punishments prescribed in the Quran for specific offences like theft, zina, and false accusation.

question_data.question: The Zakat and Ushr Ordinance was enacted in:
question_data.explanation: The Zakat and Ushr Ordinance 1980 provides for compulsory collection and distribution of zakat.

question_data.question: Waqf in Islamic law means:
options: Dedication of property for religious or charitable purposes
question_data.explanation: Waqf is the permanent dedication of property by a Muslim for religious, charitable, or pious purposes.

question_data.question: Under Islamic law, a daughter's share in inheritance when there are sons is:
question_data.explanation: Under Quranic injunctions (4:11), a daughter receives half the share of a son.

question_data.question: The Federal Shariat Court can examine whether any law is repugnant to:
question_data.explanation: Under Article 203-D, the FSC examines whether any law is repugnant to the injunctions of Islam.

question_data.question: Under the Companies Act 2017, a private company must have at least:
question_data.explanation: Section 150 requires every private company to have at least 2 directors and public companies at least 7.

question_data.question: Under Section 166 of the Companies Act, directors must act in:
question_data.explanation: Section 166 requires directors to act in good faith to promote the objects of the company.

question_data.question: SECP stands for:
options: Securities and Exchange Commission of Pakistan
question_data.explanation: The Securities and Exchange Commission of Pakistan regulates the corporate sector and capital markets.

question_data.question: Every company must hold an Annual General Meeting:
question_data.explanation: Section 85 of the Companies Act requires every company to hold an AGM each year.

question_data.question: Under the Companies Act, winding up can be:
question_data.explanation: Section 290 provides two modes of winding up: by the court or voluntary.

question_data.question: The Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act was enacted in:
question_data.explanation: PECA was enacted in 2016 to address cyber crimes in Pakistan.

question_data.question: Unauthorized access to information system under PECA Section 3 is punishable with:
question_data.explanation: Section 3 provides imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to Rs. 50,000 or both.

question_data.question: The maximum fine for cyber terrorism under PECA is:
question_data.explanation: Section 10 provides fine up to Rs. 50 million for cyber terrorism.

question_data.question: Electronic fraud is covered under which section of PECA?
question_data.explanation: Section 14 of PECA deals with electronic fraud involving unauthorized access for valuable things.

question_data.question: Offences against dignity of a natural person under PECA are in Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 16 deals with offences against the dignity of a natural person through information systems.

question_data.question: Return of income is filed under Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 114 of the Income Tax Ordinance requires every person with taxable income to file a return.

question_data.question: Capital gains are taxable under Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 37 provides that capital gains arising on disposal of capital assets are chargeable to tax.

question_data.question: Best judgment assessment can be made by the Commissioner under Section:
question_data.explanation: Section 120 allows the Commissioner to make best judgment assessment when return is not filed.

question_data.question: FBR stands for:
question_data.explanation: The Federal Board of Revenue (formerly Central Board of Revenue) administers tax laws in Pakistan.

question_data.question: The right to form trade unions is guaranteed under:
question_data.explanation: Article 17 guarantees freedom of association including the right to form trade unions.

question_data.question: Minimum wages for unskilled workers are fixed under:
question_data.explanation: The Minimum Wages Ordinance 1961 provides for fixing minimum wages for unskilled workers.

question_data.question: Under the Factories Act, the weekly holiday is:
question_data.explanation: Section 34 of the Factories Act provides that no adult worker shall work on Sunday.

question_data.question: Maximum daily working hours in a factory under the Factories Act are:
question_data.explanation: Section 36 provides no adult worker shall work more than 9 hours in any day.

question_data.question: The rule of law means:
options: Supremacy of law over arbitrary power
question_data.explanation: Rule of law means supremacy of law, equality before law, and predominance of legal spirit.

question_data.question: Which is a primary source of law in Pakistan?
question_data.explanation: Legislation enacted by Parliament is a primary source of law.

question_data.question: The doctrine of stare decisis means:
question_data.explanation: Stare decisis is the principle of following precedent - courts follow principles established in earlier decisions.

question_data.question: The two principles of natural justice are:
options: Audi alteram partem and nemo judex in causa sua
question_data.explanation: The two principles are: hear the other side (audi alteram partem) and no one should be judge in own cause (nemo judex in causa sua).

question_data.question: The literal rule of interpretation requires:
question_data.explanation: The literal rule requires words to be given their ordinary, plain, and natural meaning.

question_data.question: The maxim 'Ignorantia juris non excusat' means:
options: Ignorance of law is no excuse
question_data.explanation: Ignorantia juris non excusat means ignorance of law is no excuse - every person is presumed to know the law.

question_data.question: In jurisprudence, equity means:
options: Justice based on fairness and conscience
question_data.explanation: Equity is the body of law developed to mitigate the rigors of common law, based on fairness and conscience.

question_data.question: Obiter dicta in a judgment refers to:
options: Incidental remarks not essential to the decision
question_data.explanation: Obiter dicta are observations made by the court that are not essential to the decision and are not binding.

question_data.question: Ratio decidendi means:
options: The reason for the decision (binding part)
question_data.explanation: Ratio decidendi is the legal principle upon which the decision is based and forms binding precedent.

question_data.question: Legal positivism is associated with which jurist?
question_data.explanation: John Austin is the founder of legal positivism, defining law as a command of the sovereign backed by sanction.

study_content.title: Constitutional Law - Fundamental Rights Overview
study_content.content:
Fundamental Rights (Articles 8-28)

The Constitution of Pakistan 1973 guarantees fundamental rights in Part II, Chapter 1.

Key Articles:

  • Article 8: Laws inconsistent with fundamental rights are void
  • Article 9: Security of person - no deprivation of life or liberty except in accordance with law
  • Article 10: Safeguards as to arrest and detention
  • Article 10A: Right to fair trial (inserted by 18th Amendment)
  • Article 14: Inviolability of dignity of man and privacy of home
  • Article 17: Freedom of association
  • Article 19: Freedom of speech
  • Article 25: Equality of citizens
  • Article 25-A: Right to education (5-16 years)

Enforcement:

Under Article 199, any citizen can approach the High Court for enforcement of fundamental rights through writ jurisdiction. Under Article 184(3), the Supreme Court may take up matters of public importance involving fundamental rights.

study_content.title: Criminal Procedure - Bail Essentials
study_content.content:
Bail Provisions under CrPC

Types of Bail:

  1. Pre-arrest bail (Section 498 CrPC): Anticipatory bail granted before arrest
  2. Post-arrest bail (Section 497 CrPC): Granted after arrest in non-bailable offences
  3. Bail in bailable offences (Section 496): As of right

Key Principles:

  • Bail is the rule, jail is the exception
  • Reasonable grounds to believe accused not guilty
  • Further inquiry into guilt is required
  • Accused is not previously convicted of similar offence

Cancellation of Bail:

Section 497(5) CrPC allows cancellation when accused misuses bail or tampers with evidence.

study_content.title: Contract Act 1872 - Key Concepts
study_content.content:
Contract Act 1872 - Essential Concepts

Formation (Section 10):

Free consent + competent parties + lawful consideration + lawful object = valid contract.

Definitions:

  • Offer (S.2a): Proposal to do or abstain
  • Acceptance (S.2b): Signifying assent to proposal
  • Consideration (S.2d): Quid pro quo
  • Void (S.2g): Not enforceable

Vitiating Factors:

  • Coercion (S.15), Undue Influence (S.16), Fraud (S.17), Misrepresentation (S.18), Mistake (S.20-22)

Types of Contracts:

  • Indemnity (S.124), Guarantee (S.126), Bailment (S.148), Agency (S.182)

study_content.title: Evidence Law - QSO Key Provisions
study_content.content:
Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984

Structure:

The QSO replaced the Indian Evidence Act 1872, incorporating Islamic principles of testimony.

Key Concepts:

  • Article 17: Competence and number of witnesses per Islamic injunctions
  • Article 27: Admissions
  • Article 37: Dying declarations
  • Article 59: All facts except document contents provable by oral evidence
  • Article 117: Burden of proof on the person who asserts
  • Article 153: Estoppel

Examination of Witnesses:

  • Examination-in-chief (Art. 132): By the party calling the witness
  • Cross-examination (Art. 133): By the adverse party
  • Re-examination (Art. 134): By the calling party

study_content.title: PPC - Offences Against Body (Qisas and Diyat)
study_content.content:
Pakistan Penal Code - Qisas and Diyat Law

Key Sections (299-338):

  • S.299: Definitions - badl-i-sulh, daman, diyat
  • S.300: Qatl-i-amd (intentional murder)
  • S.302: Punishment - death as qisas, life imprisonment, or 25 years as ta'zir
  • S.308: Diyat - minimum 30,630 grams of silver
  • S.309: Qatl-bis-sabab (unintentional)
  • S.310: Qatl-i-khata (by mistake)
  • S.316: Hurt defined
  • S.324: Attempt to commit qatl-i-amd

Waiver and Compounding:

Under Qisas and Diyat provisions, the heirs of the victim may waive or compound the right of qisas (except in fasad-fil-arz cases). The Criminal Law Amendment Act 2016 restricted compounding in honour killing cases.

study_content.title: Family Law - Marriage and Divorce
study_content.content:
Muslim Family Law in Pakistan

Marriage (Nikah):

  • Offer (ijab) and acceptance (qabul) required
  • Two Muslim witnesses mandatory
  • Dower (mahr) must be fixed
  • Registration under MFLO Section 5 compulsory

Divorce:

  • Talaq: Husband's right, notice to Chairman required (S.7 MFLO), effective after 90 days
  • Khula: Wife's right to dissolution by returning dower
  • DMMA 1939: Judicial dissolution on grounds like cruelty, desertion, non-maintenance
  • Tafwiz: Delegated right of divorce to wife

Maintenance:

Husband is bound to maintain wife. Under S.9 MFLO, wife can apply to Chairman for maintenance determination.

Custody (Hizanat):

Mother has right of custody: sons until 7 years, daughters until puberty (Hanafi school). Welfare of child is paramount (GWA S.17).

study_content.title: Transfer of Property Act - Key Concepts
study_content.content:
Transfer of Property Act 1882

Types of Transfer:

  • Sale (S.54): Transfer of ownership for a price
  • Mortgage (S.58): Transfer of interest to secure payment
  • Lease (S.105): Transfer of right to enjoy for rent
  • Gift (S.122-123): Voluntary transfer without consideration, immovable must be registered
  • Exchange: Mutual transfer of ownership

Important Doctrines:

  • Lis Pendens (S.52): Property in suit cannot be transferred to defeat rights
  • Part Performance (S.53-A): Protection of transferee who has performed part of contract

study_content.title: Criminal Law - FIR to Trial Process
study_content.content:
Criminal Justice Process

1. FIR (S.154 CrPC):

First Information Report recording cognizable offence. Non-registration is itself an offence.

2. Investigation (S.156-173):

Police investigation including examination of witnesses (S.161), recording confessions before Magistrate (S.164), and submission of challan (S.173).

3. Cognizance (S.190):

Magistrate takes cognizance on complaint, police report, or information.

4. Trial:

Charge framing, prosecution evidence, statement of accused (S.342), defence evidence, arguments, judgment.

5. Appeal:

Appeal lies to Sessions/High Court. Revision under S.435/S.439.

Key Rights:

  • Right to bail (S.496-498)
  • Right to counsel (Art. 10 Constitution)
  • Right to fair trial (Art. 10-A Constitution)

study_content.title: CPC - Suit Procedure
study_content.content:
Civil Suit Procedure Under CPC

1. Institution (S.26):

Every suit is instituted by presentation of a plaint.

2. Written Statement:

Defendant files written statement within 30 days (Order VIII).

3. Issues:

Court frames issues on material propositions of fact or law on which parties disagree (Order XIV).

4. Discovery and Inspection:

Parties may seek discovery of documents (Order XI).

5. Trial and Evidence:

Examination, cross-examination, and re-examination of witnesses.

6. Judgment and Decree (S.33):

Court pronounces judgment; decree follows.

7. Execution (S.47):

Decree executed through the court that passed it.

8. Appeals:

  • First appeal (S.96) from original decree
  • Second appeal (S.100) to High Court on questions of law
  • Revision (S.115) to High Court

study_content.title: Constitutional Law - 18th Amendment Impact
study_content.content:
18th Amendment (2010) - Major Changes

Key Reforms:

  1. Article 10-A: Right to fair trial added as fundamental right
  2. Article 19-A: Right to information added
  3. Concurrent List abolished: 47 subjects devolved to provinces
  4. Article 25-A: Right to free education (5-16 years)
  5. PM Powers: Strengthened parliamentary system
  6. Judicial appointments: Judicial Commission established (Art. 175-A)
  7. NFC Award: Enhanced provincial financial autonomy
  8. Senate Chairman: Acting President role confirmed

Provincial Autonomy:

The 18th Amendment significantly enhanced provincial autonomy by abolishing the Concurrent Legislative List and devolving subjects like education, health, and environment to provinces.

study_content.title: PECA 2016 - Cyber Crimes Overview
study_content.content:
Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act 2016

Key Offences:

  • S.3: Unauthorized access (3 months/Rs.50,000)
  • S.4: Unauthorized data copying (6 months/Rs.100,000)
  • S.5: System interference (damage to IT systems)
  • S.10: Cyber terrorism (14 years/Rs.50 million)
  • S.13: Electronic forgery
  • S.14: Electronic fraud
  • S.16: Offences against dignity
  • S.18: Offences against modesty
  • S.20: Offences against minors
  • S.21: Child pornography
  • S.24: Spamming

Investigation:

FIA (Federal Investigation Agency) is the primary investigating agency for cyber crimes.

study_content.title: NAB Law - Anti-Corruption Framework
study_content.content:
National Accountability Ordinance 1999

Key Provisions:

  • S.4: NAB established for corruption eradication
  • S.9: Corruption offences defined - gratification, misappropriation, assets beyond means
  • S.10: Punishment up to 14 years and fine
  • S.14: Power to freeze property during investigation
  • S.15: Voluntary return of assets
  • S.24: Plea bargain provisions

Criticism and Reforms:

The NAB Ordinance has been controversial. The Supreme Court has set guidelines for bail in NAB cases and emphasized that NAB should not be used for political victimization.

study_content.title: Limitation Act 1908 - Essential Guide
study_content.content:
Limitation Act 1908

Purpose:

Prescribes time limits for filing suits, appeals, and applications. Based on the principle that law helps the vigilant, not the sleeping.

Key Provisions:

  • S.3: Bar of limitation - suit after prescribed period shall be dismissed
  • S.4: If period expires on holiday, suit can be filed next working day
  • S.5: Extension for sufficient cause in appeals
  • S.12: Day from which period is reckoned is excluded
  • S.14: Time in bona fide proceedings in wrong court excluded

Common Periods:

  • Money recovery: 3 years
  • Specific performance: 3 years
  • Possession of immovable property: 12 years
  • Appeal from decree: 30/90 days depending on court

study_content.title: Companies Act 2017 - Directors and Governance
study_content.content:
Companies Act 2017 - Corporate Governance

Directors:

  • Public company: minimum 7 directors
  • Private company: minimum 2 directors
  • Independent directors required for listed companies
  • S.166: Duties - act in good faith, avoid conflict of interest

Meetings:

  • S.85: AGM mandatory every year
  • EGM can be called by directors or 10% members

Accounts and Audit:

  • S.204: Proper books of account at registered office
  • S.223: Annual audit by appointed auditor

Winding Up:

  • S.290: By court or voluntary
  • S.301: Court winding up for inability to pay debts

study_content.title: Income Tax in Pakistan - Overview
study_content.content:
Income Tax Ordinance 2001 - Key Concepts

Heads of Income:

  1. Salary (S.11)
  2. Income from Property (S.15)
  3. Income from Business (S.18)
  4. Capital Gains (S.37)
  5. Income from Other Sources (S.39)

Filing:

  • S.114: Return of income mandatory for taxable persons
  • S.120: Best judgment assessment for non-filers
  • S.122: Amendment of assessments within 5 years

Appeals:

  • Commissioner (Appeals) under S.127
  • Appellate Tribunal under S.131
  • High Court on question of law

study_content.title: Labour Laws - Workers' Rights
study_content.content:
Labour Laws in Pakistan - Key Protections

Working Conditions:

  • Factories Act: Maximum 9 hours/day, Sunday rest
  • Payment of Wages Act: Timely payment, limited deductions

Social Security:

  • EOBI: Old-age pensions and benefits
  • Workers' Welfare Fund: Housing, education, welfare
  • Social Security: Medical care, cash benefits

Trade Unions:

  • IRA 2012: Freedom of association, collective bargaining
  • CBA certification for collective bargaining
  • Protection against unfair labour practices

Dispute Resolution:

Industrial disputes resolved through conciliation, arbitration, or Labour Court adjudication.

study_content.title: Legal Ethics and Professional Conduct
study_content.content:
Legal Ethics for Advocates

Key Duties:

  • Duty to the court: honesty, candour, and respect
  • Duty to client: competence, diligence, and confidentiality
  • Duty to opponent: fairness and professional courtesy
  • Duty to profession: maintain dignity and reputation

Legal Practitioners Act 1973:

  • Registration with Bar Council mandatory
  • Misconduct punishable by disciplinary committee
  • Advocate cannot appear without vakalatnama

Bar Council Rules:

  • No touting or advertising
  • No conflict of interest
  • Maintain client privilege
  • Pro bono service encouraged

study_content.title: LLB Part I - Constitutional Law 2024
study_content.content:
LLB Part I - Constitutional Law

Annual Examination 2024

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Part A - MCQs (20 Marks)

  1. The Preamble declares Pakistan as: a) Secular Republic b) Islamic Republic c) Federal Republic d) Democratic Republic
  2. Fundamental Rights are in: a) Part I b) Part II Chapter 1 c) Part III d) Part IV
  3. Article 10-A guarantees: a) Right to education b) Right to fair trial c) Right to property d) Right to assembly
  4. The 18th Amendment was passed in: a) 2006 b) 2008 c) 2010 d) 2012

Part B - Short Questions (40 Marks)

  1. Define Parliamentary Sovereignty under the Constitution.
  2. Explain the procedure of amendment under Article 239.
  3. Discuss grounds for disqualification under Article 63.
  4. What is the significance of Article 10A?

Part C - Essay Questions (40 Marks)

  1. Critically analyze the role of the Supreme Court in protecting fundamental rights through Article 184(3).
  2. Discuss the impact of the 18th Amendment on federalism.

study_content.title: Bar Council Exam - Criminal Law 2023
study_content.content:
Bar Council Licensing Examination

Criminal Law & Procedure - 2023

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Section A (30 Marks)

  1. Define Qatl-i-amd under PPC. What punishments are prescribed?
  2. Explain the right of private defence under PPC Sections 96-106.
  3. Discuss bail provisions in non-bailable offences under Section 497 CrPC.

Section B (40 Marks)

  1. Draft an application for pre-arrest bail under Section 498 CrPC.
  2. Explain the procedure for FIR registration and its evidentiary value.
  3. Differentiate between cognizable and non-cognizable offences.

Section C (30 Marks)

  1. A is charged with murder of B. The only evidence is a dying declaration by B. Discuss admissibility.
  2. Discuss the procedure from FIR to challan under CrPC.

study_content.title: LAT Sample Paper 2024
study_content.content:
HEC Law Admission Test (LAT)

Sample Paper 2024

Time: 2 Hours | Total MCQs: 100

Section 1: Legal Aptitude (40 Questions)

  1. The rule of law means: a) Government by laws b) Government by people c) Supremacy of law d) All of the above
  2. Which organ interprets the Constitution? a) Legislature b) Executive c) Judiciary d) Military
  3. Age of majority under Majority Act 1875: a) 16 b) 18 c) 21 d) 25
  4. The highest court of Pakistan is: a) High Court b) FSC c) Supreme Court d) District Court

Section 2: Pakistan Studies (30 Questions)

  1. Lahore Resolution was passed in: a) 1938 b) 1940 c) 1942 d) 1946
  2. First Constituent Assembly head: a) Liaquat Ali Khan b) Quaid-e-Azam c) Khawaja Nazimuddin d) Bogra

Section 3: English (30 Questions)

Reading comprehension and grammar...

study_content.title: LLB Part II - Contract Law 2024
study_content.content:
LLB Part II - Contract Law

Annual Examination 2024

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Part A (20 Marks)

  1. Define 'contract' under Section 2(h) of the Contract Act.
  2. What is consideration? Can a contract exist without consideration?
  3. Distinguish between void and voidable contracts.
  4. What is fraud under Section 17?

Part B (40 Marks)

  1. Explain the essential elements of a valid contract under Section 10.
  2. Discuss the effect of agreements in restraint of trade (S.27).
  3. Explain the law of indemnity and guarantee with examples.

Part C (40 Marks)

  1. A enters into a contract with B under coercion. Discuss the legal position.
  2. Discuss the remedies available for breach of contract under the Contract Act.

study_content.title: LLB Part I - Evidence Law 2024
study_content.content:
LLB Part I - Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984

Annual Examination 2024

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Part A (30 Marks)

  1. Define 'evidence', 'fact', and 'relevant' under Article 2 of QSO.
  2. Explain Article 17 regarding competence of witnesses.
  3. Discuss the burden of proof under Articles 117-118.

Part B (40 Marks)

  1. Explain the law of dying declarations under Article 37 of QSO.
  2. What is estoppel? Discuss Article 153.
  3. Discuss the admissibility of electronic evidence in Pakistani courts.

Part C (30 Marks)

  1. A is charged with murder. The prosecution relies on circumstantial evidence. Discuss the evidentiary principles.

study_content.title: LLB Part III - CPC 2024
study_content.content:
LLB Part III - Code of Civil Procedure 1908

Annual Examination 2024

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Part A (20 Marks)

  1. What is res judicata under Section 11?
  2. Explain the difference between first appeal (S.96) and second appeal (S.100).
  3. What are inherent powers under Section 151?

Part B (40 Marks)

  1. Explain the procedure for institution and trial of a civil suit.
  2. Discuss the revisional jurisdiction of the High Court under Section 115.

Part C (40 Marks)

  1. A files a suit for recovery of money against B. B contends the suit is barred by res judicata. Discuss.
  2. Draft a plaint for a suit for specific performance of contract.

study_content.title: LLB Part II - Family Law 2024
study_content.content:
LLB Part II - Muslim Family Law

Annual Examination 2024

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Part A (30 Marks)

  1. What are the essentials of a valid Muslim marriage?
  2. Explain the different types of divorce in Muslim law.
  3. Discuss the concept of dower (mahr) and its types.

Part B (40 Marks)

  1. Explain the provisions of Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 regarding talaq.
  2. What are the grounds for dissolution of marriage under DMMA 1939?
  3. Discuss the law of custody (hizanat) of children in Muslim law.

Part C (30 Marks)

  1. A Muslim wife seeks khula from her husband. Discuss the legal procedure and requirements.
  2. Discuss the rules of inheritance under Muslim personal law.

study_content.title: Bar Council - Civil Procedure 2023
study_content.content:
Bar Council Licensing Examination

Civil Law & Procedure - 2023

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Section A (30 Marks)

  1. Explain the doctrine of res judicata with leading case law.
  2. Discuss the revisional jurisdiction under Section 115 CPC.
  3. What is the difference between an order and a decree?

Section B (40 Marks)

  1. Draft a written statement in a suit for recovery of possession.
  2. Explain the execution proceedings under CPC.

Section C (30 Marks)

  1. A obtains a decree against B. B's property is in a different jurisdiction. Discuss the execution procedure.

study_content.title: LLB Part III - Transfer of Property 2024
study_content.content:
LLB Part III - Transfer of Property Act

Annual Examination 2024

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Part A (30 Marks)

  1. Define 'transfer of property' under Section 5 TPA.
  2. Distinguish between sale, mortgage, and lease.
  3. Explain the law of gift (hiba) under Muslim law and TPA.

Part B (40 Marks)

  1. Discuss the different types of mortgages under Section 58.
  2. Explain the doctrine of lis pendens under Section 52.

Part C (30 Marks)

  1. A mortgages property to B. A fails to redeem. Discuss B's rights and remedies.

study_content.title: CSS Law Optional 2024
study_content.content:
CSS Competitive Examination

Law Optional Paper 2024

Time: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100

Part I (50 Marks)

  1. Discuss the concept of judicial review in Pakistan with reference to Articles 184 and 199 of the Constitution.
  2. Analyze the evolution of fundamental rights jurisprudence in Pakistan.
  3. Discuss the independence of judiciary as guaranteed by the Constitution.

Part II (50 Marks)

  1. Explain the concept of due process and its application in Pakistani courts.
  2. Discuss the role of the 18th Amendment in strengthening provincial autonomy.
  3. Analyze the balance between national security and fundamental rights with reference to anti-terrorism legislation.
